title: How to Take Notes When Listening and Writing Both Feel Impossible
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You are sitting in a lecture hall. The professor is speaking at a steady pace, moving through slides, explaining concepts. You are trying to take notes. But your ADHD brain drifts whenever the lecture slows down, and suddenly you have missed a key point. Your dyslexic brain struggles to get words onto the page quickly enough, and what you do write is full of spelling errors and incomplete sentences. By the end of the lecture, your notes are a mess of fragments, gaps, and indecipherable scribbles. You leave feeling like you attended the lecture and learned nothing. Standard note-taking assumes you can listen, process, and write simultaneously without difficulty. When you have ADHD and dyslexia, that assumption collapses. Here are alternative methods that reduce the cognitive load.

Why Traditional Note-Taking Fails

Note-taking is a complex cognitive task. You must listen to the speaker, filter relevant from irrelevant information, hold the key points in working memory, translate them into your own words, and produce written output, all while continuing to listen to the next sentence. Each of these steps is demanding for any brain. For the ADHD-dyslexic brain, the demands multiply.

ADHD impairs the filtering step. Your brain struggles to distinguish signal from noise. Everything the professor says feels equally important, or nothing does. Working memory limitations mean you lose the thread while trying to capture the previous point. Dyslexia impairs the translation and production steps. Converting a spoken idea into written words is slower and more error-prone. The result is that your cognitive resources are spent on the mechanics of note-taking rather than on understanding the material. You leave with pages of notes you cannot read, and a head full of fragments you cannot assemble.

The solution is not to try harder at traditional note-taking. It is to reduce the number of cognitive tasks you are performing simultaneously. Separate listening from recording. Outsource the mechanics so your brain can focus on comprehension.

Strategy One: Record First, Process Later

If your university provides lecture recordings, use them. This is the single most impactful accommodation for the ADHD-dyslexic combination. When you know the lecture is being recorded, you can focus entirely on listening and understanding during class. You can ask questions. You can participate in discussion. Your notes can be minimal: key terms, questions that arise, moments you want to revisit. The detailed notes come later, from the recording, at your own pace.

If recordings are not provided, request them as an accommodation. Many disability services offices will approve lecture recording as an accommodation for students with ADHD, dyslexia, or both. You may be asked to sign an agreement that you will not share the recordings. This is standard. Sign it and use the accommodation.

When you process the recording later, use the pause button aggressively. Pause after each major concept. Write your notes in your own words. Rewind and replay anything that was unclear. You are no longer racing to keep up with the speaker. You are in control of the pace. This transforms note-taking from a frantic chase into a deliberate study activity.

Strategy Two: Outsource the Writing

Handwriting during a lecture is often the bottleneck. Your hand cannot keep up with your brain, and your brain cannot keep up with the professor. Eliminate handwriting during class. Type your notes if typing is easier for you than handwriting, which is often true for dyslexic students. Spelling errors can be corrected later. The goal during the lecture is to capture content, not polish output.

If typing is also difficult, use dictation. Open a speech-to-text app on your laptop or phone and let it capture what the professor is saying. The transcription will be imperfect. Technical terms may be garbled. But you will have a searchable text record of the lecture that you can clean up later. The cognitive load of listening and understanding is separated from the cognitive load of writing.

Some students use a combination. They listen and jot down only key terms and questions. After class, they run the recording through a transcription app to get a complete text. They then annotate the transcription with their own comments and explanations. This workflow uses the recording to handle capture, the transcription to handle writing, and their own brain to handle understanding. Each tool does what it does best.

Strategy Three: Use Visual Note-Taking

Not all notes need to be written in sentences. For many ADHD-dyslexic students, visual formats are more natural and more effective. Mind maps place the main topic in the center and branch outward with related concepts. Flowcharts show processes and sequences. Sketches and diagrams capture spatial relationships that paragraphs cannot.

Visual note-taking bypasses some of the dyslexic barriers around written language production. You are not constructing sentences. You are mapping relationships. For ADHD, the visual format provides a more engaging canvas. The act of drawing connections and adding color keeps your brain stimulated while you process the lecture content. The resulting notes are often more useful for review than pages of linear text, because the spatial layout helps your brain retrieve the information later.

You do not need artistic skill. Stick figures, boxes, arrows, and labels are sufficient. The goal is not a beautiful diagram. The goal is a visual representation of how ideas connect. A messy mind map that captures the structure of the lecture is more valuable than a neatly written page of notes that you mindlessly transcribed.

Strategy Four: Use Skeleton Notes

If your professor provides lecture slides in advance, use them as a framework. Print the slides or open them on a tablet. Annotate them during class. Add details the professor says that are not on the slides. Mark concepts that confuse you. Highlight the most important points. The slides provide the structure. Your annotations provide the substance. You are not starting from a blank page. You are filling in gaps in an existing framework.

If slides are not provided in advance, ask for them. Some professors are willing to share slides before class as an accommodation. Even if they are not, you can often download slides after class and use them for review. Combine the slides with your minimal in-class notes and the recording to build a complete set of notes during your post-lecture review session.

The Bottom Line

Traditional note-taking demands simultaneous listening, processing, and writing, which overwhelms the ADHD-dyslexic brain. Break the tasks apart. Record lectures so you can separate listening from note production. Use speech-to-text to outsource the writing. Try visual formats that bypass sentence construction. Use skeleton notes from lecture slides to reduce the blank-page burden. The goal is not to take notes the way everyone else does. The goal is to build a system that helps you learn the material.
